reasoning: The informal statement introduces two functions f (even) and φ (odd), but the conclusion "φ[φ(x)] is an odd function" only involves φ; f appears nowhere in the conclusion. The hypothesis "f is even" is therefore superfluous — it can always be satisfied (e.g., by f ≡ 0), so the two statements are logically equivalent: ∀φ, φ odd → φ∘φ odd iff ∀f∀φ, (f even ∧ φ odd) → φ∘φ odd. The Lean definitions of OddFunction and EvenFunction (φ(-x) = -φ(x) and f(-x) = f(x)) are the standard mathematical definitions. The Lean formalization correctly captures the actual mathematical content ("odd composed with odd is odd") by keeping the relevant hypothesis on φ and omitting the irrelevant one on f. Note that EvenFunction is defined but unused, which correctly reflects that the claim does not depend on f at all.
